chunks.content:
Let’s break it down with my project (Flask application for house price prediction)
I built a very basic flask application for predicting house price based on house area; here area is user input and price is output of
Machine Learning model.
Before that, let’s clear Docker fundamentals
What is Docker?
Docker is a tool used to create containers (isolated environments) by running an image (via a Dockerfile) for our projects, including all
the files and dependencies needed to run the application.
It simplifies the deployment and production of applications. Let’s take a look at how it works.
Terminologies
Container: An isolated environment with all dependencies and data installed; think of it as a separate operating system dedicated to
running a specific application.
Image: A blueprint of instructions required to set up that container.
Dockerfile: A file used to write the instructions that must be executed before the container is created.
Relation of Container, Image and Dockerfile
Let’s understand this with a well-known concept in development programming — guess what?
Exactly, OOPs!
Think of the Dockerfile as the code written in a class, the Image as the class itself, and the object we create to use the class’s
functionalities is the Container.
See it through a python code
class Image:

def Dockerfile():
       """ Dockerfile instructions """

return run_instructions
Container = Image()
Note:
Before moving forward, you need to set up Docker Desktop by referring to Docker Desktop and make sure you have launched your
Docker Desktop for further processing.
Dockerfile
This is a file that contains instructions to complete all prerequisites before running our application. It holds only instructions.
The filename should be exactly Dockerfile, as shown below:
Let’s understand instructions one by one:
Before that, just imagine we are setting up all these requirements for running our flask application.

FROM python:3.12-slim
This instruction sets the base image with the specified Python version. Here, I’m using Python 3.12, so I used 3.12-slim. You can
change it to any Python version you need.
Now that we have our Python environment, the next step is to create folders inside the container.
WORKDIR /app
This instruction creates a folder named app inside the container where we will perform all our operations. Essentially, we are
packaging the Flask application inside the app folder.
COPY requirements.txt /app/
This copies the requirements.txt file into the container, specifically into the app folder.
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
This installs all the Python libraries and dependencies in the container environment.
So far, we’ve set up the Python environment, created the app folder, copied requirements.txt into it, and installed all necessary
libraries. Now, it’s time to add the rest of the files into the container using: COPY . /app/
ENV FLASK_APP=app
This helps the Docker daemon locate your application inside the container to run it.
EXPOSE 8000
This sets the port inside the container that will communicate with your local port.
CMD [“flask”, “run”, “ — host”, “0.0.0.0”, “ — port”, “8000”]
This is the command used to run our Flask application from within the container.
This is all about Dockerfile. Now let’s move on to build a Docker image from the Dockerfile.
Docker Image
Once your Dockerfile is complete, you’re ready to build a Docker image from it.
The instruction below is used to run a Docker image:
After building the image, it’s time to build a container from the image.
Container
To build a container, we need to run the image we just built. Let’s do it!
Here’s what the parameters mean:
•
-d: Runs the container in detached mode.
•
-p: Specifies the port number.
•
5000:8000: Maps port 5000 on the local machine to port 8000 in the container.
•
my_app:first: my_app is the application name, and first is the tag.
Why use port 8000 for this container?
In the Dockerfile, we specified EXPOSE 8000, meaning the container will use port 8000 for data exchange.

To verify that your application is running on port 5000 (the local machine port), go to http://localhost:5000/. There, you should see your
application in action.
Result
You can see your running container on Docker Desktop
Conclusion
Docker simplifies the process of building, deploying, and scaling applications by using containerization. By following the steps outlined
in this guide, you’ve learned how to create a Dockerfile, build an image, and run a container for a Flask application. This approach
ensures that your application runs consistently across different environments, making it ideal for production deployment.
To experiment further, feel free to check out my Docker and GitHub repositories:
- GitHub Repo: House Price Prediction Flask Application Git Repo
- Docker Hub Repo: House Price Prediction Flask Application Docker Repo
Happy experimenting!

-2
Correlation (Bivariate analysis)
This is the bivariate analysis term used to summarize the relation between two columns in a single number that can range from +1
to -1.
•
positive correlation (correlation > 0)
•
negative correlation (correlation < 0)
We can say there is a positive correlation if both the column values are increasing and decreasing simultaneously. I mean, if the
column 1 value is high, then the column 2 value must be high, and vice versa.
HP and Car Weight shows positive correlation.
library(corrplot)
cor(data$hp, data$wt)
0.658747887344759
In the negative correlation column, 1 and 2 will show inverse behavior; if the column 1 value is high, then the column 2 value will be
low, and vice versa.
HP and Mileage shows negative correlation
cor(data$hp, data$mpg)
-0.776168371826586
The correlation metric is commonly used to summarize the correlation for multiple columns as shown below.
library(corrplot)
cor_matrix = cor(data)
corrplot(cor_matrix)
Multiple Column Correlation
Bivariate Visualization (Numerical Vs Numerical)
1. scatterplot
2. Hexagonal binning
3. Contour plot
These plots are more descriptive than correlation and correlation metrics; you can actually see the influence of one variable on another
by visualizing them.
A scatter plot is made to visualize small datasets; it plots a single data point per instance. If your data contains 100000 rows, then you
can expect the same number of dots in 2D if you are plotting for two columns.

library(ggplot2)
ggplot(diamonds, aes(x = carat, y = price)) +
 geom_point(
   color = "blue",
   alpha = 0.2,
   size = 0.8
 ) +
 labs(
   title = "Scatterplot: Diamond Carat vs Price",

color = "blue",
   alpha = 0.2,
   size = 0.8
 ) +
 labs(
   title = "Scatterplot: Diamond Carat vs Price",
   subtitle = "Individual observations showing relationship between carat and price",

alpha = 0.2,
   size = 0.8
 ) +
 labs(
   title = "Scatterplot: Diamond Carat vs Price",
   subtitle = "Individual observations showing relationship between carat and price",
   x = "Carat",

y = "Price"
 ) +
 theme_minimal()
Scatterplot
Why is a scatterplot not enough? — Scatterplots are showing each datapoint with dots, so if you are dealing with huge data, you
probably see a crowded graph that tells you nothing about the data, although you can adjust the transparency of the dots to see the
dense area, but it is not enough if the data is very large.
A hexagonal binning overcomes the scatterplot limitation by creating same-shape hexagons to show the correlation.
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(diamonds, aes(x = carat, y = price)) +
 geom_hex() +
 labs(

title = "Hexbin Plot: Diamond Carat vs Price",
   x = "Carat",
   y = "Price",

fill = "Count"
 ) +
 theme_minimal()
Hexabin Plot
Note: Hexagonal binning is not plotting one hexagon per datapoint; instead, it will create a beehive-type structure and use the
darkness to show the data density.
Why is hexagonal binning not enough? — If you see, I have plotted the same data using a scatterplot and hexagonal binning. Although
hexagonal binning is able to explain the near intercept (0, 0) data, it is hugely dense but still much less informative.
A contour plot overcomes this problem by plotting edges of dense areas, or contours of dense areas, to show more details, as you can
see in the image below.
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(diamonds, aes(x = carat, y = price)) +
 geom_density_2d() +
 labs(
   title = "Contour Plot: Diamond Carat vs Price",
   subtitle = "Density contours showing concentration of observations",

title = "Contour Plot: Diamond Carat vs Price",
   subtitle = "Density contours showing concentration of observations",
   x = "Carat",

y = "Price"
 ) +
 theme_minimal()
ContourPlot
So in the contour plot, you can tell that other than the intercept (0, 0), there are 2–3 denser spots.
Bivariate Visualization (Categorical Vs Numerical)
•
Box Plot
•
Violin Plot
To compare the categorical data on some numeric values as a data scientist, you mostly use box plots.
boxplot(price ~ clarity, data = diamonds)
Diamond Clarity Type Vs Price (Box Plot)
In summary of the box plot
1. Clarity type “IF” is the cheapest compared to others.
2. The “SI2” category holds very high pricing if you compare the 50th percentile / median.
3. “VS2” and “VS1” are very similar.
4. Long whiskers indicate that the data is more spread out and not tightly concentrated around the median.
Why are box plots not enough? — Here box plots are not showing me the density of data with respect to price.
Violin plots came to solve the density problem as shown below.
ggplot(diamonds, aes(clarity, price)) + geom_violin()
Diamond Clarity Type Vs Price (Violin Plot)
In summary of the violin plot:
1. Most of the “IF” category data lies in the lower price range.
2. A large chunk of the “SI2” category is ranging around the 5000 price, so this is the most expensive category.
3. “VS2” and “VS1” are very similar.
4. If you compare the “I1” and “SI2” categories from the 5000 to 10000 price range, “I1” is higher in volume than “SI2,” which means in
this range “I1” can be more expensive than “SI2.”
Point 4 will be very difficult to track with box plotting, but the violin plot shows that information to us very clearly.
So thanks to all these correlations and bivariate analysis techniques for contributing to data science.

Correlation Isn’t Enough: Understanding Data Relationships Through Bivariate Analysis—DS-2 was originally published in GoPenAI on
Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

Location Estimates — Mean
Mean is just the average of data points, right? But do you know there are three different types of mean?
•
Mean
•
K% trimmed mean
•
Weighted mean
The simple mean is the sum of elements divided by the number of elements. The interesting concepts are the K% trimmed mean and
weighted mean, so let’s discuss them one by one.
K% trimmed mean:
This mean is introduced to reduce the effect on the mean due to outliers in the data. K% refers to how much percent of the data should
be trimmed from the head and tail of the sorted sequence.
Where to use? — If we have data with outliers, then we can simply trim these outliers from the lower and upper bounds to compute a
more trustworthy mean.
# Regular mean of population
regular_mean <- mean(data$Population..2020.)
# K% trimmed mean of population
trimmed_mean <- mean(data$Population..2020., trim=0.1)
cat("regular mean ", regular_mean, end="\n")
cat("trimmed mean ", trimmed_mean, end="\n")
regular mean

33227444
trimmed mean

11326178
A comparison of the regular mean with the 10% trimmed mean shows that there are some outliers in the data, and these outliers are
creating this difference.
Weighted mean:
Weighted mean gives us more control over individual elements from the data; with weighted mean, we can decide the importance of
each element.
Where to use? — Let’s consider you are dealing with sensor data, and you know there are 2–3 sensors with high tolerance (error rate).
In this condition, you can assign low weight/importance to these sensors to compute a more trustable mean.
# weighted mean of population (world share is used as weights)
weights <- sapply(data$World.Share, function(x) as.numeric(trimws(gsub("%", "", x))))
weights <- unname(weights)
weighted_mean <- weighted.mean(data$Population..2020., w=weights)
cat("weighted mean", weighted_mean)

weighted mean 582250341
So here we have used world share as weights for the population.
Location Estimates — Median
•
Median
•
Weighted median
Median is another location estimation metric, which is basically the middle number of a sorted sequence; it divides the data perfectly
with the same length on both sides.
In weighted median, we use weights instead of elements of array to get that middle number from the array of weights.
Where to use? — Suppose you have data on every Indian state’s median salary, and now you want to compute the median salary of
India. Then you have to use the weight of each state according to their population.
High population = high trust = high weight and vice versa.
Estimates of variability
•
MAD (mean absolute deviation)
•
Standard Deviation
•
Variance
These metrics tell us how much you can trust the mean.
Mean absolute deviation is the sum of the absolute difference of each element with the mean/average of the sequence.
cat("MAD - Mean Absolute Deviation ", mad(data$Density..P.Km..), end="\n")
MAD - Mean Absolute Deviation

103.782
Variance is the squared version of MAD; we take the square of the difference instead of the modulus. Mostly we prefer variance over
MAD because dealing with squares is easy in calculus as compared to modulus.
Standard deviation is the square root of variance. SD is more reliable because it comes in the same scale.
cat("std of Density ", sd(data$Density..P.Km..), end='\n')
cat("variance of Density ", var(data$Density..P.Km..), end='\n')
std of Density

2331.286 
variance of Density

5434894
IQR (interquartile range) tells us more detailed information about data spread.
IQR = Q3 — Q1
Here Q3 is the 75th percentile and Q1 is the 25th percentile, so IQR is the middle 50% range, mostly used for outlier detection and
box plots.

Outlier Detection with IQR
•
Lower bound = Q1–1.5 * IQR
•
Upper bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
Below Lower bound and above upper bound data points are outliers
# Basic boxplot with detailed title
boxplot(df$hp,

main = "Boxplot of Horse Power",
       ylab = "Horse Power",
       col = "lightblue",
       border = "darkblue",
       notch = TRUE)

ylab = "Horse Power",
       col = "lightblue",
       border = "darkblue",
       notch = TRUE)   # shows confidence interval around median
# Calculate quartiles and IQR
Q1

col = "lightblue",
       border = "darkblue",
       notch = TRUE)   # shows confidence interval around median
# Calculate quartiles and IQR
Q1  <- quantile(df$hp, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)
Q3

<- quantile(df$hp, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)
Q3  <- quantile(df$hp, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
Median <- median(df$hp, na.rm = TRUE)
IQR_value <- IQR(df$hp, na.rm = TRUE)
# Add text to plot
text(1.2, Q1,

paste("Q1 =", round(Q1,2)), pos = 4)
text(1.2, Median, paste("Median =", round(Median,2)), pos = 4)
text(1.2, Q3,

paste("Q3 =", round(Q3,2)), pos = 4)
# Print IQR in console
cat("IQR =", IQR_value, "\n")
cat("Lower Bound =", Q1 - 1.5 * IQR_value, "\n")
cat("Upper Bound =", Q3 + 1.5 * IQR_value, "\n")
Box plot with IQR

This article takes you through the complete lifecycle of a Machine Learning project. From ETL to deployment, I’ll share every detail of
how I brought this project to life.
Data Engineering
Data Engineering serves as the starting point in the Machine Learning project lifecycle, bringing all distributed data together in
one place.
For this project, I utilized an ETL pipeline — a core concept in Data Engineering. It enabled me to extract raw data, transform it into a
meaningful format, and load it into a suitable location for further processing.
Let’s deep dive into ETL pipeline
ETL stands for Extract, Transform, and Load. These pipelines can be executed periodically (e.g., daily or hourly) to fetch real-time
data, enhancing the predictive power of our Machine Learning model for real-world applications.
This process can be automated using tools like Apache Airflow, Kubeflow Pipelines, AWS Step Functions, and more, streamlining the
workflow for consistent and efficient data updates.
ETL Pipeline:
Extract Operation
The extract operation is responsible for fetching data from various sources such as websites, databases, and APIs, as illustrated in the
flowchart above.
Transform Operation
The transform operation focuses on data cleaning, feature selection, and manipulation. In my project, I used this step to extract only
the required features, ensuring the data is ready for the next phase.
Load Operation
The load operation transfers the transformed data to its destination, where we can choose the appropriate storage format. Typically,
the processed data is stored in a database for further analysis and model training.
For my project, I used PySpark to build the ETL pipeline, as it enables efficient processing of large datasets.
Why not Pandas?
While Pandas is excellent for small to medium-sized datasets, it stores DataFrames in RAM, which can lead to out-of-memory
exceptions when handling large datasets.
In contrast, PySpark creates a session and processes data in chunks, storing it in ROM, making it ideal for handling large-scale data.
For the actual implementation of the pipeline using PySpark, please refer to the accompanying jupyter notebook.
EDA (Data Analysis)
Data understanding is a critical stage before building any Machine Learning model. It allows us to analyze the data, plan the data
processing steps, and gain insights into its structure and quality.
In this project, I examined the balance of data in my training and testing datasets and found it to be well-distributed across all four
categories.
Throughout the entire process, I focused on two columns:
1. Tweets
2. Sentiments
The Tweets column contains the raw Twitter text data, while the Sentiments column serves as the target variable for prediction.

Natural Language Processing
I applied several key NLP techniques to preprocess the data and prepare it for Machine Learning model building. Below are the main
steps I executed:
Text Processing
•
Regex: I applied regular expressions to clean the text by removing URLs, hashtags, HTML tags, and keeping only alphanumeric
characters. This helped eliminate unnecessary noise from the data.
•
NLTK: Using the Natural Language Toolkit (NLTK), I performed word tokenization, stemming and lemmatization.
Word tokenization is just splitting sentence into words, so we can processing each word from sentence individually.
Stemming helps us to truncate prefix or suffix of text to reduce count on unique words from corpus (paragraph).
Lemmatization is the proecss of converting any word into it’s base word, for eg. Played will convert to play.
WordCloud
Word cloud concept help you to understand importance of words from given data. I splitted my data into four sections.
•
data for negative sentiments
•
data for positive sentiments
•
data for neutral sentiments
•
data for irrelevant sentiments
Here is the representation of most frequent words for each category.
Here you can clearly see that there is no much difference in negative and positive sentiments data.
this is representation of bad data, here we can filterout our data for further processing, it might reduce data but you can do data
augmentation techniques here to increase your data.
augmenting of data in NLP with TF-IDF will not bad idea because TF-IDF and any other porcessing technique that I used is not able to
detect sentence grammer or it doesn,t require sophisticated text.
You can think our input text will work as bag of word for model. you can understand by refering below vectorization technique.
Vectorization with TF-IDF
I used Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) for text vectorization. This method transforms text into a numerical
format, considering the importance of each word across documents, which prevents frequent words from dominating the model.
TF-IDF stands for Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency.
Term Frequency (TF): This measures how frequently a term (t) appears in a specific document (d). It’s calculated by dividing the
number of occurrences of the term in the document by the total number of terms in that document.
Inverse Document Frequency (IDF): This measures how important a term is across the entire collection of documents. It’s calculated
by taking the logarithm of the ratio of the total number of documents (N) to the number of documents containing the term (df(t)). A
higher IDF value indicates a rarer term, making it more significant.
Here is the resulant data we got from TF-IDF
After all preprocessing of text it’s time to save our data for mlflow experiments. To see actual implementation of end to end process of
NLP you can refer jupyter notebook.
mlflow experiments
MLflow experiments allow you to conduct multiple experiments with your trained model, helping you track and compare results over 
time. For running MLflow experiments, I prefer using Databricks as it offers an integrated experiment section, making the process

much more streamlined and efficient.
In Databricks, you can easily connect your experiment by passing the experiment ID into the MLflow code. This integration simplifies
the entire workflow, enabling better experiment tracking and easier comparison of model performance.
For my experiments, I worked with several models, including Logistic Regression, Multinomial Naive Bayes, and Decision Tree
Classifier. By applying different combinations of parameters, I was able to experiment with and compare the performance of each
model. Here’s a preview of the Logistic Regression model’s F1 score, which highlights the model’s ability to balance precision
and recall:
Logistic Regression F1 Score: 0.58
This approach allowed me to track the effectiveness of each model and make adjustments as needed for improving performance.
F1 score with different parameters (Logistic Regression)
I recommend running the notebook below in your own account to see the results. You’ll definitely start appreciating the power
of MLflow.
For more information, please refer to the accompanying Databricks notebook.
Hyperparameter tuning
Hyperparameter tuning is crucial for identifying the best parameter combination for your model. This technique involves an iterative
process where, for every parameter combination, the model is trained and tested on a dataset. It is often described as a “trial and
error” method.
However, this approach can be computationally expensive, especially when working with complex or heavy machine learning models.
For large-scale problems, hyperparameter tuning can be made more efficient through sampling or batch methods. In these methods,
you don’t use the entire dataset for training the model; instead, you choose random or stratified data points from the dataset to train
the model. Although this may slightly reduce accuracy, it is more feasible when working with large datasets.
For my project, I used Grid Search CV to find the best hyperparameter combination for the model. Below are some common
techniques for hyperparameter tuning, especially for large datasets:
1. Grid Search This technique exhaustively searches through a specified set of hyperparameter values, trying all possible
combinations. While effective, it can be computationally expensive for larger datasets due to the exhaustive nature of the search.
2. Random Search Randomly samples from the hyperparameter space, offering a faster alternative to grid search. This method
explores a wider range of hyperparameters with fewer evaluations, making it more efficient for larger datasets.
3. Bayesian Optimization This method uses probabilistic models to predict the performance of different hyperparameters. It selects
the next set of hyperparameters to evaluate based on previous results, making it more efficient and suitable for large datasets.
4. Genetic Algorithms Inspired by natural selection, these algorithms iteratively evolve a population of hyperparameter sets to improve
model performance. This method works well with complex search spaces.
5. Hyperband Hyperband combines random search with early stopping to dynamically allocate resources across multiple
configurations, identifying promising hyperparameters quickly without excessive computational costs.
6. Bayesian Optimization with Gaussian Processes This technique models the hyperparameter search space using Gaussian
processes, focusing on regions that are likely to yield better results, which is particularly useful for large datasets where
computational resources are limited.
To optimize hyperparameter tuning for larger datasets, these techniques can be combined with parallel computing and distributed
processing frameworks such as Dask, Spark, or multi-GPU setups. This enables more efficient hyperparameter search and reduces
the overall computational overhead.
I choosed Logistc Regression model and trained my Model with best parameters.
Source Distribution for Model Packaging
Packaging your machine learning model is a best practice, especially if you don’t plan to update it frequently. Imagine thousands of
lines of code that can now be utilized with just a single line — this is the power of model packaging.

1. Project Folder Setup
To ensure better organization, I created a main folder called sentiment_prediction and moved all machine learning pipeline files and
dependencies into this folder. This helped in maintaining a clean structure and simplified the management of the entire project.
Before moving forward I recommend you to visit this pdf it will practically show you step by step process for building python package.
PDF : step by step guid for python package building
2. Manifest.in
The Manifest.in file plays a crucial role in controlling which files and folders should be included or excluded during the packaging
process. It helps to specify the structure of the package for distribution.
Key commands used in the Manifest.in file include:
•
include <file/folder>: Include specific files or folders.
•
exclude <file/folder>: Exclude specific files or folders.
•
recursive-include <path>: Include all files from a directory recursively.
•
recursive-exclude <path>: Exclude all files from a directory recursively.
3. Setup.py
The setup.py file contains the project’s metadata and is essential for creating the package. It defines key information about the project,
such as:
•
Project name, version, description, and author details.
•
Dependencies required for the package (install_requires), making it easy to install all necessary libraries.
4. Building the Package
To build the package, I used the following command:
python setup.py sdist bdist_wheel
This command generates two folders:
•
build/: Contains the entire project package as defined in the Manifest.in.
•
dist/: Contains the distributable files: .whl (wheel file) .gz (compressed source archive)
5. Global Access via GitHub
Now you can access your package gloablly, by refering your repository. I provided my package below go and check out.
•
Repository: GitHub Repo Link
You can install the package directly from GitHub using the following command:
pip install git+https://github.com/vijaytakbhate2002/sentiment_prediction_python_package.git
To ensure it worked globally, I tested it again:
from sentiment_prediction import predict
print(predict.predictor("Great progress shared today!"))

output:['Negative']
Flask Application
Building application will help us to give our NLP model experience to people, so I built one flask application.
Web Application UI
Here is demonstration of project: project demo
I left a blank section for user suggestion and feedback, these feedbacks are getting stored in database for future model analysis or any
business work.
Database configuration
For storing collected user data we need to configure a database. It will help us to improve model as per user need.
I used Google Cloud MySQL instance for integrating my application with database, GCP is paid but you can use free credit of GCP for
first 3 months, for doing almost all Cloud Work.
You need to create your GCP account, then create one instance under SQL and by configuring your local system with instance you
are good to go.
Docker containerization
If you are not awared about docker and it’s basic concepts you can refer my previous article which explains you all about docker.
This guide will help you build a solid foundation in Docker, enabling you to confidently use it for your projects.
Docker guide: Comprehensive Docker guide for deploying Flas app
Deploy
Deployment of web app will help us to engange people and provide them real actual experience of our services.
After deployment you need to collect user data and store it for future analysis, this data contain user feedback and suggestions.
After deployment it’s not end of the process we need to collect user feedback and again follow same steps fine tune Model as per
user need.
Summary of Blog
This blog covers the lifecycle of a Machine Learning project, from ETL to deployment. It details building an ETL pipeline using PySpark
for efficient data handling, EDA, and NLP preprocessing techniques like tokenization, TF-IDF, and WordCloud visualization.
It highlights ML experiments with MLflow on Databricks and hyperparameter tuning using Grid Search.
The model was packaged into a Python package and deployed as a Flask application with a database backend (Google Cloud
MySQL) and Dockerized for scalability.
The app collects user feedback for continuous improvement. It emphasizes end-to-end integration, including cloud and
containerization, to deliver a robust ML solution.
Happy Learning!

This blog covers all about model evaluation metrics for classification problems. Before diving into metrics, let’s discuss types of
classification in Machine Learning.
Types of Classification
These models are designed to predict a category for a given input. The predicted category is one of the classes present in the
training dataset.
Types of Classification:
1. Single-Class Classification A classifier that predicts only one class for each input is called a single-class classifier.
2. Multi-Class Classification A classifier that predicts multiple classes for an input is called a multi-class classifier.
Model Evaluation Metrics for Classifiers
Model evaluation metrics help us understand the prediction accuracy of a model for each class. Due to data imbalance, classifiers
often perform well for some classes but fail for others.
To identify such behaviors, the following metrics are used:
•
Accuracy
•
Precision
•
Recall
•
F1 Score
•
Confusion Matrix
•
ROC AUC Curve
Before diving into matrics you need to understand basic’s about some terminologies.
The prediction class for which we are calculating matrics is called positive (wanted prediction) class and remaining classes will be
negative (Unwanted Prediction).
True refers to accurate prediction by model and False refers to inaccurate prediction by model.
TP : True Positive (Accurate wanted prediction)
FP : False Positive (Inaaccurate wanted prediction)
TN : True Negative (Accurate unwanted prediction)
FN : False Negative (Inaccurate unwanted prediction)
Now let’s understand all above matrics one by one
Accuracy
Accuracy is measure of rate of correct predcitions out of all inputs to model, accuracy tells you how many predictions are correct out of
you provided to model.
Here you can see all TP + TN which means all Accurate predicions, it don’t care about individual predictions.
Accuray will not give you complete understanding of model performance it’s just overall performance of model.
But what if you want to know accuracy for perticular class from prediction, and this curiosity brings new matrics called precision.
Precision
Model accuracy for a particular class is nothing but the precision for that class. Precision indicates how well our model is performing for
a specific class.

Precision
Prediction, also called accuracy of positive predictions, is measured using precision, which is calculated for each class in the dataset.
Now, what happens if a Data Scientist uses a small amount of data for model evaluation? For example, let’s say they test the model
with 3 data points:
•
Out of these, 1 data point is positive and is predicted correctly.
•
The other 2 data points are negative and predicted incorrectly.
Here,
•
True Positive (TP) = 1
•
False Positive (FP) = 0
Even though two predictions are wrong, we get:
Precision=TP / (TP + FP) = 1 / (1 + 0) = 1 = 100% Precision
This result is clearly misleading, right?
To address this issue, a new metric was formulated, called Recall.
Recall
To avoid issue with only precision mostly we are prefer use precision and recall both at a time. Recall is responsible to calculate True
Positive rate of our model and also called sesitivity matrics.
Let’s take previous senario where Data Scienctist testing model with 3 data points, we are taking same numbers here
•
Out of these, 1 data point is positive and is predicted correctly.
•
The other 2 data points are negative and predicted incorrectly.
Here,
•
True Positive (TP) = 1
•
False Positive (FP) = 0
•
False Negative (FN) = 2
Even though two predictions are wrong, we get:
Recall=TP / (TP + FN) = 1 / (1 + 2) = 0.33 = 33% Recall
This result clearly indicates that the recall of this model is very low.
Recall helps guide the Data Scientist to take action before deploying the model. But again, we get 100% precision and 33% recall. So,
how can we combine these two metrics into a single number that summarizes both precision and recall?
This leads to the introduction of a new metric called the F1 Score.
F1 Score
F1 score is just combined result of Precision and Recall, take a look at below formula of F1 Score.
F1 Score
F1 Score for our problem : 2 * (100 * 33) / (100 + 33) = 49.62%
Confusion Matrix

Confusion matrix is a way of representing model predictions for each class with two axis true axis and predicted axis.
confusion matrix
Here, you can see that there are 0, 1, and 2 classes. The diagonal shows all correct predictions, which include both True Positives
(TP) and True Negatives (TN). The area of the diagonal is darker, representing larger numbers, such as 4.
The cells representing a value of 1 indicate misclassifications. For example, if the actual class is 1, but the model predicts it as class 0,
that would be a misclassification.
ROC AUC Curve
ROC stands for Receiver Operating Characteristics, and AUC stands for Area Under the Curve. The ROC curve plots the True
Positive Rate (TPR) against the False Positive Rate (FPR) at various threshold settings.
This distinguishes it from the F1 Score, which provides a single value by considering a threshold of 0.5 in binary classification. In
contrast, the ROC curve allows you to evaluate the model’s performance across all possible threshold values.
Here, you can see that the True Positive Rate (TPR), also known as Recall, increases with an increase in the False Positive
Rate (FPR).
For a perfect classifier, the AUC will be 1, whereas for random predictions, the AUC will be 0.5. In the graph, the two axes represent
TPR and FPR, both ranging from 0 to 1, forming a square with a side length of 1.
The area of the square is:
are of square
If the AUC occupies the entire area (a rare scenario), the model is considered perfect.
That’s all about Machine Learning metrics, folks! Let’s summarize it.
Summary for the Blog
This blog comprehensively explores model evaluation metrics for classification problems in machine learning. It begins by introducing
the two main types of classification — Single-Class Classification (where one class is predicted per input) and Multi-Class
Classification (where multiple classes can be predicted).
The core focus lies in explaining essential evaluation metrics that help assess and improve the performance of classification models.
These metrics address common challenges such as data imbalance and misclassification. Key highlights include:
1. Accuracy: Measures the overall correctness of predictions but lacks insights into individual class performance.
2. Precision: Indicates the accuracy of positive predictions, focusing on a specific class.
3. Recall (Sensitivity): Evaluates the model’s ability to identify all positive instances.
4. F1 Score: Combines Precision and Recall into a single metric to balance their trade-offs.
5. Confusion Matrix: Visualizes model predictions for all classes, highlighting both correct and incorrect classifications.
6. ROC AUC Curve: Evaluates the model’s performance across all possible thresholds, providing a holistic view of True Positive Rate
(TPR) vs. False Positive Rate (FPR).
The blog emphasizes practical examples and scenarios to demonstrate how these metrics work and why they are essential for
identifying a model’s strengths and weaknesses.
By the end of the blog, readers will understand the nuances of these metrics and their importance in building and deploying effective
machine learning classifiers.
Thanks for reading!
Happy Learning!

There are several techniques for ML training. Among these, I will cover the following:
•
Supervised and Unsupervised Learning
•
Semi-Supervised Learning
•
Offline and Online Learning
•
Instance-Based and Model-Based Learning
Supervised and Unsupervised Learning
Supervised learning is like spoon-feeding our ML model in its initial stages, allowing it to learn and improve over time. Here, I’m
referring to the training process.
In supervised learning, there are input columns and output columns, also called target columns. For example, in spam detection — a
classification problem — the input is the email, and the target is whether the email is spam or not. That’s it!
This process resembles a student-teacher scenario where the teacher is a human, and the student is the model. The dataset serves
as the knowledge used to train the student (model).
In Unsupervised learning, we are not aware of the data labels. Instead, we separate the input data by analyzing similarities and
grouping them into different clusters.
Once the clusters are formed, we can assign custom labels to each one. This technique is widely used to identify product relationships
in online shopping and to recommend new products based on a user’s purchase history.
Here three clusters with three different image categories are formed
Semi-Supervised Learning
Semi-Supervised Learning is a combination of supervised and unsupervised learning, where some data is labeled and some data is
unlabeled. A good example of this is Google Photos, which automatically separates new photos into their respective groups based on
whether they contain a particular person in each image.
There are several techniques under semi-supervised learning; here, we will focus on the following:
•
Self Learning
•
Consistency Regularization
•
Generative Models
•
Graph-Based Learning
Let’s discuss them one by one:
Self Learning
Self Learning trains a model with labeled data and generates pseudo-labels for the unlabeled data by prediction.
Then, the model is trained on the entire dataset, which includes both the generated pseudo-labels and the labeled data.
Consistency Regularization
This technique uses data augmentation to generate similar data, and then the model is enforced to predict the same outcome for both
the augmented and original data.
It helps create a model that can find similarities in both labeled and unlabeled data, predicting the same output for unlabeled data as it
would for labeled data.
Data augmentation includes techniques like image flipping, blurring, rotation, etc. After augmenting the data, the model is trained to
predict the same class for both the augmented and original images.
Generative Models

Generative models create synthetic data points and learn the underlying structure of the training data. These models can generate
new datasets using encoders.
Examples include Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) and Variational Autoencoders (VAEs).
Graph-Based Learning
This technique creates nodes for both labeled and unlabeled data. Similar nodes are then connected to each other.
For example, when dealing with images, each image starts as a separate node. If similar images are found based on their features,
they are grouped or connected by an edge.
Refer below graph to understand graph-based learning
Here label 1 is came from labeled image of group 1, simillarly for label 2 and label 3. Finally we came up with three labels that means
labeled data has three unique labels.
Batch (Offline) and Online Learning
Batch Learning
Batch learning, also known as offline learning, is a technique where the developer needs to stop the deployed ML model, retrain it on
new data, and then redeploy it.
This technique is useful when frequent updates are not required. For example, product recommendation systems can often be
retrained weekly or even over several days.
Batch learning can be costly when training the model on the entire dataset every time. However, using incremental learning models
can help avoid unnecessary retraining, making the process more efficient.
Here are some examples on incremental learning models, as of now we are focusing on general concepts so don’t go much deeper
into these types.
•
Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
•
Naive Bayes (Online Version)
•
Incremental Support Vector Machines (ISVM)
•
Online Random Forests
•
Incremental Decision Trees
You can refer this article to learn more about incremental learning
Here’s your text with grammatical corrections and improved clarity:
Online Learning
Online learning is used when an ML model needs to be updated continuously with new inputs, such as in stock price prediction. In this
scenario, the model must remain aware of the most recent data.
Only models capable of incremental learning are used in online learning. Each new instance is fed back into the model to update its
internal weights based on the latest input.
Instance-Based and Model-Based Learning
Instance-Based Learning
Instance-based learning involves comparing a new input with stored data. If similarity is found, the model returns the label of the
corresponding input. This approach uses pattern matching techniques.
Here model works as a search engin not exactly same but it will find simmilarity from stored data.

Some common models used in instance-based learning include:
•
K-Nearest Neighbors (K-NN) with labels
•
Locally Weighted Regression, etc.
Model-Based Learning
In model-based learning, we train the model using a training dataset. During the training process, the model creates its own complex
patterns (mathematical equations) to make predictions for future inputs.
A simple example is the equation y=mx+cy = mx + c, which represents a trained model. Some common models used in model-based
learning include:
•
Linear Regression
•
Logistic Regression
•
Decision Tree, etc.
Summary
Machine Learning techniques can be broadly categorized into supervised, unsupervised, and semi-supervised learning.
Supervised learning uses labeled data, like spam detection, while unsupervised learning works with unlabeled data, grouping similar
points into clusters.
Semi-supervised learning combines both, employing techniques like self-learning and consistency regularization to leverage partially
labeled datasets.
Other approaches include batch learning, where models are retrained periodically, and online learning, which updates models
continuously with new data, suitable for dynamic tasks like stock price prediction.
Instance-based learning relies on pattern matching (e.g., K-Nearest Neighbors), while model-based learning creates mathematical
models (e.g., Linear Regression) to make predictions.
This is all about Machine Learning techniques. If you learned something, let me know in the comments. Your suggestions will help me
improve my blogs.
Thanks for reading!
Supervised, Unsupervised, and Beyond: ML Techniques Simplified was originally published in GoPenAI on Medium, where people are
continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

What is a matrix?
The two-dimensional array with rows and columns is called a matrix. It represents real value at each location, and the location can be
denoted as a variable. ‘ i ‘, ‘ j ‘ . Here ‘ i ‘ indicates rows and ‘j’ indicates columns.
The above matrix shows a, b, c, and d are elements can be any real number of the matrix; each element has a unique address, and
the address indicates with variables i and j as discussed above.
A[i][j] = A[0][0] = a; first row and first column.
A[i][j] = A[0][1] = b; first row and second column.
A[i][j] = A[1][0] = c; second row, first column.
A[i][j] = A[1][1] = c; second row, second column.
Why is matrix?
We understood what a matrix is; now it’s time to see why a matrix is there. Let’s dive into it.
Think about a scenario where you have an Excel sheet with salaries and the number of leaves mentioned in it. Let’s suppose your
manager told you to add 1000 extra salaries for each of them by reducing the number of leaves by 1 for each of them.
Definitely, you will do it just by an excel formula = SUM(col, val)
Here the action you performed is called matrix addition; it seems very simple; wait, this is not enough about matrix.
So a matrix is to apply mathematical operations to two-dimensional data.
I found an awesome article that guides you to understand arithmetic operations and some information about vectors as well.
Read this article before proceeding = Matrix arithmetic operations
Note: Before proceeding, I recommend you go through the above article, which prepares you for upcoming advanced topics.
Matrix For Machine Learning
Machine learning is a tool to deal with data to learn from it and produce future trends and outcomes. There is only one way to deal with
massive data, and this is a matrix.
Topics Covered
1. Transpose of matrix
2. Inverse of matrix
3. Determinant of matrix
4. Eigenvectors and Eigenvalues of matrix
You will learn these topics with an application-oriented mindset without further delay. Let’s get started.
Transpose of matrix
The transpose of a matrix is nothing but altering rows into columns and columns into rows.
Data Science and Machine Learning : Transposing matrices helps in performing operations like covariance calculation, where you
need to align data vectors for computation.
Computer graphics: The transpose is crucial in transforming coordinates, such as rotating or scaling objects in 3D space.
Indian air pollution satellite image
An Indian corporate team is working to build a deep learning model for level of pollution prediction through satellite images. And for this
project, they scrapped images from the internet. Let’s assume this scenario.

But unfortunately, they didn’t collect enough images to train a better CNN (Convolutional Neural Network).
Now guess how to solve this issue?
Exactly! by transpose matrix, an image is nothing but a multidimensional array with pixel values in it, right? In this case, matrix
transpose will rotate the image by a 90° angle and create another image.
This process of generating images by using referring available images is called the Data Augmentation technique.
In Machine Learning it’s common to work with matrix and vector, especially finding weights, which is the key concept. So let’s
understand it with one example.
Let’s learn it through one example below, Keep in mind we have y value and X value; our goal is to find out w
Determinant of matrix
In three-dimensional space, think about a cube with coordinates (0,0,0), (0,0,1), (0,1,0), and (1,0,0).
Original Cube
The original cube in 3D space has the following coordinates for its vertices:
•
X Y Z
•
0 0 0
•
0 0 1
•
0 1 0
•
1 0 0
Transformation
The cube is stretched along the y-axis by 1 additional unit. This means the new coordinates are:
•
X Y Z
•
0 0 0
•
0 0 1
•
0 2 0 (changed)
•
1 0 0
How much new volume is a multiple of the previous cube?
Obviously, the new volume is 2 cubic units bigger than the previous one, right?
Let’s prove it with determinant:
Transformation Matrix T
The transformation matrix T is designed to:
•
Leave x-axis and z-axis values unchanged (1 on the diagonal for x and z).
•
Scale the y-axis by 2 (placing 2 in the middle of the diagonal to stretch the y-values).
Thus, the transformation matrix becomes:
Determinant of matrix T
So, to determine the change in volume, we need all the stretched vectors ( eigenvectors) and the amount by which each vector is
stretched ( eigenvalues).
Eigenvectors and Eigenvalues of matrix

Definition: The vectors that get stretched during transformation of any volume without changing the direction are called Eigenvectors.
The unit by which the vector gets stretched is called Eigenvalues.
In summary, matrices are powerful tools for handling and manipulating multidimensional data, making them essential in various fields
such as Machine Learning, Data Science, and computer Graphics.
Throughout this article, we explored key concepts, including the transpose, inverse, determinant, and eigenvectors and eigenvalues of
matrices.
These fundamental operations enable us to perform transformations, solve equations, and understand changes in space, such as
volume transformations. They also play a crucial role in tasks like data augmentation in deep learning and in building predictive models
by calculating key parameters such as weights.

Breakdown
Statistical inference is a powerful technique used to make predictions or draw conclusions about an entire population based on data
collected from a smaller sample of that population.
Two types of statistical inference
1. Parametric inference
2. Hypothesis testing
Let’s understand statistical inference with a very relatable incident for all of us, and that is the COVID-19 pandemic situation.
How was statistical inference used during the pandemic?
During the COVID-19 pandemic, researchers conducted numerous experiments to develop effective vaccines. After rigorous testing
and analysis, they ultimately recommended two or three vaccines for widespread public use.
This raises an important question: How did researchers select these two or three vaccines after all the experimentation, even though
they had never been tested on the entire global population? How could they predict how the vaccines would perform for everyone?
Oh, that sounds interesting, right? Let’s find out how?
Parametric inference
During the pandemic, it was not possible to test each vaccine on every person on Earth. So the question arises: How did researchers
arrive at their conclusions?
Now here is parametric inference rising. Let’s dive in and see how it will work for this problem.
They picked random people from around the globe. This sample group consists of individuals from various regions, so their lifestyles
and immune systems are quite distinct from one another.
Now, they will conduct experiments on these people, and this sample data serves as a hypothetical representation of the entire world’s
population. This type of experiment is called parametric inference.
Parametric inference
Hypothesis testing
Hypothesis testing is a probabilistic and statistical approach for drawing conclusions or making decisions about certain events.
In our case, let’s suppose researchers have developed two vaccines, but they want to select only one to distribute globally, ensuring it
is highly effective for people. This decision will be based on data obtained through hypothesis testing.
Vaccine selection depends on the time required to create antibodies for COVID-19.
Before proceeding, let’s understand the terms which we are going to use in future calculations
•
Null hypothesis: H0 (Assumption that vaccine one and vaccine 2 are both equally effective)
•
Alternate Hypothesis: H1 (Proving that null hypothesis is wrong)
•
Fail to reject: if H1 can’t prove that H0 is wrong for some experiments, then this condition called failed to reject.
Results of experiments on the sample group of people are as follows:
Hypothesis testing on sample people groups
•
Blue group = Vaccine A
•
Red group = Vaccine B

In Experiment 1, Vaccine B took less time, so H0 was rejected by H1. Now, H0 is updated with the current scenario (Vaccine B is more
effective than Vaccine A).
In Experiment 2, Vaccine B took less time, so H0 was rejected by H1. Now, H0 is updated with the current scenario (Vaccine A is more
effective than Vaccine B).
In Experiment 3, Vaccine B took less time, but this time H0 failed to reject by H1. Now, H0 is the same as before.
So by doing repetitive experiments, we are ensuring the fairness of the experiment. In our example, it was difficult for researchers to
tell one better vaccine by just doing experiments on one sample group of people.
Lets understand it in brief with another example of coin toss fairness
If suppose 50 times you got head on 100 experiments that shows equal probabilities for head and tail, and that’s perfect.
What if we get 30 times head on 100 experiments? That may not be fair. You can say here this experiment is not fair or the
experimental tool (coin) has some issue with it, right?
Let’s wind it up with the following terminologies:
Confidence interval: The boundaries that are defined by consultation of domain experts for taking decision of fairness are called
Confidence intervals; in our case, it might be 30 to 70%.
Significance value: Total area of graph (confidence interval); you can say tails area of this bell-shaped graph is significance value.
Conclusion
- Definition: Statistical inference allows conclusions about a population based on sample data.
- COVID-19 Example: Researchers used this technique to select effective vaccines without testing on the entire global population.
- Methods:
- Parametric Inference: Conducted experiments on diverse sample groups to generalize results.
- Hypothesis Testing: Compared vaccine effectiveness, leading to informed decisions on distribution.
- Key Concepts:
- Confidence Interval: Boundaries defining fair decision-making (e.g., 30% to 70%).
- Significance Value: Area in the tails of the distribution, indicating statistical significance.
This concise approach underscores the importance of statistical inference in making impactful decisions.
